categories.name: Factual error
categories.description: A checkable statement of fact that is simply wrong — wrong date, wrong place, wrong person, wrong number.
categories.example: "Taxila was a university" — it was a famed hub of individual teachers; the institutional-university model belongs to later Nālandā.

categories.name: Stripped context
categories.description: A real quote or fact presented without the surrounding text, genre conventions, or historical setting that changes its meaning.
categories.example: A single Manusmṛti verse presented as 'the' Hindu position, with the text's contested authority and adjacent verses removed.

categories.name: Mistranslation / selective scripture
categories.description: Meaning altered in translation, or one reading of a disputed term presented as the only reading.
categories.example: '33 koṭi devas' rendered as '330 million gods' — koṭi read only as 'crore', never as 'class/type'.

categories.name: Colonial-era distortion
categories.description: Framings manufactured or hardened under colonial scholarship and administration, still circulating as neutral fact.
categories.example: Caste presented as one timeless, pan-Indian, scripturally fixed system — a picture substantially shaped by colonial census and law.

categories.name: Conflation
categories.description: Two distinct things merged so a conclusion about one is smuggled onto the other: nation and civilization, founded religion and evolved tradition, text and practice.
categories.example: "This Indus seal is 2,400 years older than Hinduism" — treats an unfounded, evolved tradition as if it had a founding date.

categories.name: Misattributed origins
categories.description: A contribution assigned to the wrong culture, person, or era — in either direction.
categories.example: 'Arabic numerals' — Arabic sources themselves call the system 'Indian reckoning' (ḥisāb al-hind).

categories.name: Anachronism
categories.description: Projecting a modern category — usually a nation-state, ideology, or identity — onto a past that predates it.
categories.example: "Ancient Pakistani grammarian Pāṇini" — a 1947 state projected 2,400 years backward onto Gandhāra.

categories.name: Cherry-picking
categories.description: Real but unrepresentative evidence presented as the norm; counter-evidence within the same tradition omitted.
categories.example: Satī presented as a universal Hindu command, omitting its regional/temporal boundedness and the tradition's own opposition to it.

categories.name: Internal overclaim / pseudohistory
categories.description: Exaggerated or fabricated claims made in FAVOR of the tradition. The ledger corrects these with the same rigor as external distortions — inflated claims corrode the credible record.
categories.example: The Vaimānika Śāstra as proof of ancient aircraft — the text is early-20th-century and aerodynamically unsound.

categories.name: Fabricated evidence
categories.description: Invented quotes, doctored images, nonexistent studies, fake institutional endorsements.
categories.example: "NASA declared Sanskrit the best language for computers" — no such declaration exists; a real 1985 AI Magazine paper is inflated into a fake institutional endorsement.

categories.name: Hate speech / dehumanization
categories.description: Slurs, dehumanizing tropes, and eliminationist rhetoric. Documented as a pattern with prevalence data — distinct from criticism, which is not hate.
categories.example: Slur lexicons and 'dirty/backward' tropes tracked across platforms by network-contagion research.

categories.name: Legitimate debate (not distortion)
categories.description: Genuine scholarly disagreement. Recorded so that real scholarship is never miscategorized as attack — and so partisans on either side cannot claim it as settled.
categories.example: Indo-Aryan migration and Indus–Vedic continuity: active research with serious evidence on multiple sides.

verdictDirections.name: Overclaim
verdictDirections.meaning: Exaggeration or fabrication in favor of the tradition.

verdictDirections.name: Misrepresentation
verdictDirections.meaning: Distortion of, or appropriation from, the tradition.

verdictDirections.name: Legitimate scholarship
verdictDirections.meaning: Genuine scholarly work or open question — not distortion.

flagForOptions: Nation-state projected onto the ancient past (e.g. 'ancient Pakistan')
Heritage appropriation / severing from its living tradition
Fake quote or fake institutional endorsement (NASA, Einstein...)
Scripture quoted out of context or mistranslated
